input.prompt:
We migrated our test project from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform.
Now our CI pipeline fails with exit code 8 even though no tests actually
failed. Some test assemblies have conditional compilation and produce zero
tests on certain configurations. With VSTest this was fine. How do I fix
this behavioral difference?

data.content:
We migrated our test project from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform.
Now our CI pipeline fails with exit code 8 even though no tests actually
failed. Some test assemblies have conditional compilation and produce zero
tests on certain configurations. With VSTest this was fine. How do I fix
this behavioral difference?
data.transformedContent:
<current_datetime>2026-05-09T01:01:36.755Z</current_datetime>

We migrated our test project from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform.
Now our CI pipeline fails with exit code 8 even though no tests actually
failed. Some test assemblies have conditional compilation and produce zero
tests on certain configurations. With VSTest this was fine. How do I fix
this behavioral difference?

input.initialPrompt:
We migrated our test project from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform.
Now our CI pipeline fails with exit code 8 even though no tests actually
failed. Some test assemblies have conditional compilation and produce zero
tests on certain configurations. With VSTest this was fine. How do I fix
this behavioral difference?

data.reasoningText:
The user migrated from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform and is getting exit code 8 when test assemblies produce zero tests. This is a known behavioral difference - Microsoft.Testing.Platform returns a non-zero exit code (8) when no tests are discovered, whereas VSTest treated this as a success.

Let me look at their project files to understand the setup.

toolResult.sessionLog:

@@ -1,0 +1,2 @@
 ConditionalTests.cs
 TestProject.csproj

result.detailedContent:
diff --git a/tmp/sv-fbf1b91f11bd4d85abc95ed295c7def6 b/tmp/sv-fbf1b91f11bd4d85abc95ed295c7def6
create file mode 100644
index 0000000..0000000
--- a/dev/null
+++ b/tmp/sv-fbf1b91f11bd4d85abc95ed295c7def6
@@ -1,0 +1,2 @@
 ConditionalTests.cs
 TestProject.csproj

data.content:
Exit code **8** means "no tests discovered" in Microsoft.Testing.Platform. Since all your tests are behind `#if INCLUDE_SLOW_TESTS`, the runner finds zero tests and returns 8.

The fix is a single MSBuild property:

arguments.new_str:
<EnableMSTestRunner>true</EnableMSTestRunner>
    <TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>true</TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>
    <!-- Treat zero discovered tests as success (matches VSTest behavior) -->
    <TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests>0</TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests>

data.reasoningText:
The issue is clear. With Microsoft.Testing.Platform, exit code 8 means "no tests were found." When `INCLUDE_SLOW_TESTS` is not defined, all tests are compiled out, and the test runner returns exit code 8 instead of 0.

The fix is to add the `<TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests>0</TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests>` MSBuild property to the project file. This tells Microsoft.Testing.Platform to return exit code 0 when no tests are discovered, matching VSTest behavior.

Let me also verify this by checking if dotnet is available and building/running.

arguments.new_str:
<EnableMSTestRunner>true</EnableMSTestRunner>
    <TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>true</TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>
    <!-- Treat zero discovered tests as success (matches VSTest behavior) -->
    <TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests>0</TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests>

toolCalls.args:
{"path": "/tmp/sv-fbf1b91f11bd4d85abc95ed295c7def6/TestProject.csproj", "old_str": "    <EnableMSTestRunner>true</EnableMSTestRunner>
    <TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>true</TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>", "new_str": "    <EnableMSTestRunner>true</EnableMSTestRunner>
    <TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>true</TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>
    <!-- Treat zero discovered tests as success (matches VSTest behavior) -->
    <TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests>0</TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests>"}

toolArgs.new_str:
<EnableMSTestRunner>true</EnableMSTestRunner>
    <TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>true</TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>
    <!-- Treat zero discovered tests as success (matches VSTest behavior) -->
    <TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests>0</TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests>

data.content: The `--minimum-expected-tests` doesn't accept 0, but there's `--ignore-exit-code 8` which is exactly what we need. Let me update the project file:

arguments.old_str:
<EnableMSTestRunner>true</EnableMSTestRunner>
    <TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>true</TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>
    <!-- Treat zero discovered tests as success (matches VSTest behavior) -->
    <TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests>0</TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests>
arguments.new_str:
<EnableMSTestRunner>true</EnableMSTestRunner>
    <TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>true</TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>
    <!-- Exit code 8 = "no tests found". Treat as success to match VSTest behavior
         for assemblies that may have zero tests under certain configurations. -->
    <TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>--ignore-exit-code 8</TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>

data.reasoningText:
So `--minimum-expected-tests 0` is not valid - it requires a "non-zero positive integer". But I can see there's a `--ignore-exit-code` option:

```
--ignore-exit-code
    Do not report non successful exit value for specific exit codes
    (e.g. '--ignore-exit-code 8;9' ignore exit code 8 and 9 and will return 0 in these case)
```

So the correct fix is to use `--ignore-exit-code 8`. Let me update the project file to use this instead.

Actually, wait. Let me reconsider. The proper MSBuild-level approach might be to set `<TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>--ignore-exit-code 8</TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>` or there might be a direct property for this.


I should remove the `TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests` property since it doesn't seem to exist, and instead pass `--ignore-exit-code 8` through the `TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments` MSBuild property.

arguments.old_str:
<EnableMSTestRunner>true</EnableMSTestRunner>
    <TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>true</TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>
    <!-- Treat zero discovered tests as success (matches VSTest behavior) -->
    <TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests>0</TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests>
arguments.new_str:
<EnableMSTestRunner>true</EnableMSTestRunner>
    <TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>true</TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>
    <!-- Exit code 8 = "no tests found". Treat as success to match VSTest behavior
         for assemblies that may have zero tests under certain configurations. -->
    <TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>--ignore-exit-code 8</TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>

diff --git a/tmp/sv-fbf1b91f11bd4d85abc95ed295c7def6/TestProject.csproj b/tmp/sv-fbf1b91f11bd4d85abc95ed295c7def6/TestProject.csproj
index 0000000..0000000 100644
--- a/tmp/sv-fbf1b91f11bd4d85abc95ed295c7def6/TestProject.csproj
+++ b/tmp/sv-fbf1b91f11bd4d85abc95ed295c7def6/TestProject.csproj
@@ -1,15 +1,15 @@
 <Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">
   <PropertyGroup>
     <TargetFramework>net8.0</TargetFramework>
     <OutputType>Exe</OutputType>
     <EnableMSTestRunner>true</EnableMSTestRunner>
     <TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>true</TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>
     <!-- Exit code 8 = "no tests found". Treat as success to match VSTest behavior
          for assemblies that may have zero tests under certain configurations. -->
     <TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>--ignore-exit-code 8</TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>
   </PropertyGroup>
   <ItemGroup>
     <PackageReference Include="MSTest" Version="3.8.0" />
   </ItemGroup>
 </Project>

data.content:
**The fix:** Add `--ignore-exit-code 8` via `<TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>` in your project file.

**What's happening:** Microsoft.Testing.Platform returns exit code **8** when zero tests are discovered (unlike VSTest which returned 0). The `--ignore-exit-code 8` flag tells the runner to treat that specific exit code as success, while still properly failing on actual test failures.
